coredata.dc:title: Ultrafast generation of highly crystalline graphene quantum dots from graphite paper via laser writing
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Aitang
Chen, Tao
Song, Shangwei
Yang, Wenrong
Justin Gooding, J.
Liu, Jingquan
coredata.dc:description: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are attractive fluorescent nanoparticles that have wide applicability, are inexpensive, nontoxic, photostable, water-dispersible, biocompatible and environmental-friendly. Various strategies for the synthesis of GQDs have been reported. However, simple and efficient methods of producing GQDs with control over the size of the GQDs, and hence their optical properties, are sorely needed. Herein, an ultra-fast and efficient laser writing technique is presented as a means to produce GQDs with homogeneous size from graphene produced by the instantaneous photothermal gasification and recrystallization mechanism. Controlling the laser scan speed and output power, the yield of GQDs can reach to be about 31.458 mg/s, which shows promising potential for large-scale production. The entire process eliminates the need for chemical solvents or any other reagents. Notably, the prepared laser writing produced GQDs (LWP-GQDs) exhibit blue fluorescence under UV irradiation of 365 nm and the Commission Internationale de L’Eclairage (CIE) chromaticity coordinates is measured at (0.1721, 0.123). Overall, this method exhibits superior advantages over the complex procedures and low yields required by other existing methods, and thus has great potential for the commercial applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Laser writing
Ultra-fast
Large-scale
Graphene quantum dots
Fluorescence

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration for the synthesis of LWP-GQDs.

(a) SEM image of the laser scanning path on graphite paper, the inset represents the digital photo of the graphite paper irradiated by laser. (b) and (c) SEM images of the gathered LWP-GQDs with different magnifications. (d) TEM and (e) HRTEM images of LWP-GQDs. (f) SEM image of side section of the graphite paper used. (g) Size distribution histogram of GQDs. (h) The AFM topography image and (i) height profile of LWP-GQDs.

The chemical characterization of the LWP-GQDs (a) The XRD patterns, (b) FT-IR spectra and (c) Raman spectra of the synthesized materials. (d) Survey XPS spectrum and (e) high-resolution spectra for C 1s and (f) O 1s.

(a) The optimal excitation and emission photoluminescence spectra of LWP-GQDs, the inset photograph shows blue fluorescence irradiated by ultraviolet-light with 365 nm wavelength. (b) Emission spectra obtained at progressively increasing excitation wavelengths from 300 to 370 nm for LWP-GQDs. (c) The photoluminescence decay curves of LWP-GQDs. (d) Luminous point of LWP-GQDs on CIE 1931 chromaticity diagram.

head.ce:title: Ultrafast generation of highly crystalline graphene quantum dots from graphite paper via laser writing

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: Aitang Zhang and Tao Chen contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: An ultrafast and efficient strategy is developed to produce homogeneous GQDs through instantaneous photothermal gasification and recrystallization, revealing the promising potential for large-scale production.

Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are attractive fluorescent nanoparticles that have wide applicability, are inexpensive, nontoxic, photostable, water-dispersible, biocompatible and environmental-friendly. Various strategies for the synthesis of GQDs have been reported. However, simple and efficient methods of producing GQDs with control over the size of the GQDs, and hence their optical properties, are sorely needed. Herein, an ultra-fast and efficient laser writing technique is presented as a means to produce GQDs with homogeneous size from graphene produced by the instantaneous photothermal gasification and recrystallization mechanism. Controlling the laser scan speed and output power, the yield of GQDs can reach to be about 31.458 mg/s, which shows promising potential for large-scale production. The entire process eliminates the need for chemical solvents or any other reagents. Notably, the prepared laser writing produced GQDs (LWP-GQDs) exhibit blue fluorescence under UV irradiation of 365 nm and the Commission Internationale de L’Eclairage (CIE) chromaticity coordinates is measured at (0.1721, 0.123). Overall, this method exhibits superior advantages over the complex procedures and low yields required by other existing methods, and thus has great potential for the commercial applications.

ce:section.ce:para: Graphene is a sp2-hybridized carbon atom monolayer structure arranged in six-membered rings that has been extensively studied and applied because of its superb electrical, mechanical and thermal characteristics [1–3]. Monolayer, or several layer, graphene with dimensions of only a few nanometers are often defined as graphene quantum dots (GQDs) [4–6]. Zero-dimensional nanodots with strong quantum confinement and impressive biocompatibility hold promise for in vivo/in vitro bioimaging, biosensing, photocatalysis, optoelectronic devices and drug delivery [7–11]. Additionally, GQDs exhibit high luminescence and outstanding light stability, which overcome issues of light bleaching and blinking [3,12].

Developing simple, effective, easily scalable and environment-friendly strategies to synthesize GQDs off homogeneous size is of considerable significance [13]. Numerous strategies have been explored to generate GQDs. For instance, they can be prepared by chemical oxidation of carbon black [14], hydrothermal cracking of graphene oxides [15], electrochemical stripping of graphite [16], thermal degradation of organic molecules [17], ruthenium-catalyzed decomposition of C60 [18] and step-by-step organic synthesis [19–21]. These approaches can be roughly divided into two categories: bottom-up and top-down methodologies [13,22]. Nevertheless, while the most top-down approaches usually require hazardous chemicals and complex synthetic protocols and the bottom-up ones often time-consuming procedure [23,24]. These methods typically produce polydispersed GQDs of a variety of diameters (5–16 nm). As the optical properties can be size dependent, separation of the polydisperse GQDs is usually through gel electrophoresis, which is time consuming and produces low amounts of materials [3,25,26].

With progress in graphics and accurate electromechanical control software, laser writing has become a valuable tool for rapid, inexpensive, and non-contact fabrication of electronics compared to the traditional manufacturing processes [27–29]. Laser writing has the potential to produce novel nanostructures and devices [30]. Laser writing has been widely employed in the fabrication of micro-devices and micro-patterns as well as surface modification and material processing due to its superiority in nanoscale spatial resolution and 3D prototyping functionality, which does not need any admixtures or solvents compared to traditional processes and can be carried out at ambient temperatures [31–34]. Graphene oxide, polyimide and a variety of commercial polymers can be transformed into porous graphene films by laser irradiation, which can be used directly as binder-free electrodes for supercapacitors [27,34–38].

Herein, an ultrafast and efficient laser writing strategy is utilized to produce GQDs from graphene produced by the instantaneous photothermal gasification and recrystallization mechanism (henceforth referred to as laser writing produced graphene quantum dots or LWP-GQD) with uniform size for the first time. The whole process is easy to operate with high efficiency and requires no further chemical solvents or other reagents. The graphite paper was photothermally gasified to the gaseous pyrographite, which was subsequent recrystallized at lower temperature when the laser passed through. The as-generated LWP-GQDs showed blue fluorescence under UV irradiation at 365 nm. Overall, this strategy exhibits superior advantages over the complex procedures and low yields required by other existing methods, and thus has great potential for the commercial applications.

Schematic representation of the laser writing technique to generate GQDs is illustrated in Fig. 1 . GQDs were successfully generated using a laser writing instrument. By changing the output power of the laser, grooves with controlled depths can be obtained on the graphite paper surface. During this process, GQDs are generated on both sides of the grooves, which is attributed to the power of the laser causing photothermal vaporization and recrystallization of the graphene nanosheets. That is, graphite with tightly stacked graphene sheets is instantaneously photothermally vaporized under the laser irradiation to generate gaseous graphene. The graphene vapour, subsequently recrystallizes on both sides of the generated grooves when the laser passes that point. The generated GQDs was then collected and dispersed in aqueous solution. The resultant dispersion emits blue fluorescent light under irradiation at 365 nm. These results demonstrate a facile, ultra-fast and highly efficient methodology for production of homogeneous GQDs in large-scale [39], notably, the yield of GQDs can reach to be about 31.458 mg/s by controlling the laser scan speed and outpower intensity.

The morphology and structure of the prepared LWP-GQDs were first investigated using scanning electron microscope (SEM). By comparing the cross-sectional SEM images of graphite paper before and after laser writing, it was found that the stacked nanosheets are successfully shredded into GQDs which were gathered together. Very importantly, these gathered GQDs could be easily separated in solvents such as water as shown in Fig. 2 d. It can be clearly seen from Fig. 2a, a fracture groove was generated on the surface of pristine graphite paper by carefully tuning the laser output power density, while the GQDs were generated on both sides of the fracture groove. (As shown in the right side of Fig. 1). The length and depth of the fracture groove can be easily adjusted by the laser intensity and writing speed. In this process, the quantum dots were generated on basis of the proposed instantaneous photothermal gasification and recrystallization mechanism [40]. As shown in Fig. 2b and c, uniform GQDs were aggregated and formed a porous structure. Furthermore, after the laser irradiation, the color of employed pristine graphite paper changed from metallic silver to black, as shown in the illustrated photographic image of Fig. 2a. For comparison, SEM images of the side section of pristine graphite paper without laser irradiation are shown in Fig. 2f and Fig. S2. It can be observed that, in the absence of laser writing, the graphite paper consists of stacked graphene layers with smooth surface.

In order to further explore the crystal structure of the prepared LWP-GQDs, TEM analysis was conducted, and the corresponded images are shown in Fig. 2d. It can be observed from Fig. 2g that the prepared LWP-GQDs are well distributed with an average size of 4.5 nm. (Fig. 2g) Furthermore, the HRTEM image shown in Fig. 2e reveals its highly ordered lattice plane with an average spacing of 0.149 nm, as expected for the (003) lattice spacing of graphite. The thickness of the prepared LWP-GQDs was characterized using AFM analysis. As displayed in Fig. 2h-i and Fig. S3, the average thickness of the prepared quantum dots is measured to be about 4.5 nm. EDS analysis was employed to confirm the elemental distribution of the synthesized LWP-GQDs. From Fig. S4, it can be seen that the C and O elements are uniformly distributed on the laser-irradiated fracture surface. Moreover, the oxygen content of the laser-irradiated fracture surface area was slightly increased compared to the original graphite paper precursor. This observation can be attributed to the instantaneous photothermal decomposition of H2O molecular into oxygen atoms which subsequent bond with carbon atoms in this special condition.

XRD was then employed to investigate the crystallinity of the prepared material. From Fig. 3 a, it can be observed that the LWP-GQDs exhibit strong diffraction peaks at 26.6° and 54.8°, which match well with the (003) and (006) lattice planes of the graphite standard pattern (JCPDS No/261079), respectively. This result indicates that the prepared LWP-GQDs have good crystal structures. In addition, the surface features of the synthesized nanomaterials were then investigated by FT-IR spectroscopy, and the results are shown in Fig. 3b. The characteristic absorption peaks appeared at 1385 and 1080 cm−1 can be ascribed to the CO stretching vibration. The two weak peaks appeared at 2927 and 2852 cm−1 are ascribed to the CH bending mode. The characteristic absorption peak of the CC bond appeared at 1627 cm−1. A broad peak was observed at 3441 cm−1, which is ascribed to the OH stretching of the absorbed water. Raman spectrum of the as-generated GQDs shows a stronger G-band near 1576 cm−1, indicating their well crystallinity. This result is attributed to that the pyrogenic decomposition of the graphite paper under the photothermal gasification and the subsequent recrystallization after the laser moves past a given location. Moreover, the pyrolytic and recrystallization processes could generate enormous GQDs and cause the disorder of carbon atoms, leading to the appearance of wider d-band. Furthermore, XPS was then performed to further explore the surface elemental configurations and chemical bonding states of the LWP-GQDs. The full survey spectrum shown in Fig. 3d shows the presence of C and O elements. The high-resolution spectrum of C 1 s can be deconvoluted into three peaks at 287.9 eV, 286.3 eV, and 284.8 eV, which correspond to CO, CO, and CC/CC functional groups, respectively. Moreover, three peaks located at 532.8 eV, 531.8 eV and 531.1 eV should be attributed to O-CO, CO and CO groups. In addition, the atomic % of carbon, nitrogen and oxygen in XPS is measured to be 52.77%, 2.34% and 44.89%, respectively.

To investigate the fluorescent properties of the LWP-GQDs, their photoluminescence spectra were measured. As shown in Fig. 4 b, the LWP-GQDs exhibited different fluorescence emission intensities at diverse excitation wavelengths. The maximum excitation and emission wavelengths were measured at 342 nm and 430 nm, respectively. (Fig. 4a) Moreover, under 365 nm UV irradiation, the LWP-GQDs exhibited blue fluorescence and the coordinate on CIE chromaticity was appeared at the (0.1721, 0.123), as shown in Fig. 4d. The electrochemical emission decay is usually obtained according to the equation of

, which consists of a short-lived component τ1 of 0.83 ns and a long-lived component τ2 of 7.85 ns as shown in the inset of Fig. 4c. Based on the above values and the relationship of

)
, the average fluorescence lifetime of the LWP-GQDs was calculated to be 0.83 ns.

In summary, an ultrafast, highly efficient and easy operated laser writing strategy is reported to produce the homogeneous graphene quantum dots in large scale using graphite paper as precursor via photothermal gasification and recrystallization mechanism. The entire process is rapid, simple and efficient to produce GQDs, eliminating the need for chemical solvents and complicated procedures. Moreover, the prepared LWP-GQDs showed blue fluorescence under UV irradiation at 365 nm and the Commission Internationale de L’Eclairage (CIE) chromaticity coordinates was measured to be (0.1721, 0.123). Overall, this method exhibits superior advantages over the complex procedures and low yields required by other existing methods, and thus has great potential for the commercial applications.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by Qingdao Innovation Leading Talent Program and Double-Hundred Foreign Experts Program of Shandong Province.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jcis.2021.03.044.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Recent advances in graphene quantum dots: synthesis, properties, and applications

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Haque, E.; Kim, J.; Malgras, V.; Reddy, K. R.; Ward, A. C.; You, J.; Bando, Y.; Hossain, M. S. A.; Yamauchi, Y. Recent Advances in Graphene Quantum Dots: Synthesis, Properties, and Applications, Small Methods 2018, 2, 1800050.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Coupled quantum dots in bilayer graphene

Sapkota, B.; Benabbas, A.; Lin, H. G.; Liang, W.; Champion, P.; Wanunu, M. Peptide-Decorated Tunable-Fluorescence Graphene Quantum Dots, ACS Appl Mater Inter 2017, 9, 9378-9387.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Curvature and vacancies in graphene quantum dots

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Graphene quantum sheets with multiband emission: unravelling the molecular origin of graphene quantum dots

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Zhu, H.; Xu, Y.; Liu, A.; Kong, N.; Shan, F.; Yang, W.; Barrow, C. J.; Liu, J. sb:title.sb:maintitle: Signatures of single quantum dots in graphene nanoribbons within the quantum hall regime

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A Universal graphene quantum dot-tethering design strategy to synthesize single-atom catalysts

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Jin, S.; Ni, Y.; Hao, Z.; Zhang, K.; Lu, Y.; Yan, Z.; Wei, Y.; Lu, Y. R.; Chan, T. S.; Chen, J. A Universal Graphene Quantum Dot-Tethering Design Strategy to Synthesize Single-Atom Catalysts, Angew Chem Int Ed 2020, 59, 1-6.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Zhang, A.; Zheng, W.; Yuan, Z.; Tian, J.; Yue, L.; Zheng, R.; Wei, D.; Liu, J. Hierarchical NiMn-layered double hydroxides@CuO core-shell heterostructure in-situ generated on Cu(OH)2 nanorod arrays for high performance supercapacitors, Chem Eng J 2020, 380, 122486.
